refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two concerns: (1) the functional requirement to enable teleoperation communication, and (2) a performance constraint on latency. The refined FR isolates the core functional obligation (enable teleoperation) while deferring the quantitative performance criterion to acceptance criteria or design constraints. This keeps the FR solution-neutral and problem-focused. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' is vague and belongs in constraints; the FR now states the functional intent without prescribing how safety is achieved.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the teleoperation communication pathway is operational end-to-end (tele-operator input → remote equipment response) under nominal network conditions. Measure latency at each stage (input capture, transmission, processing, actuation feedback) to establish baseline. Verify against acceptance criterion p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall capture tele-operator control inputs.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator control inputs to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall process tele-operator control inputs at the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The NGHE solution shall execute remote equipment actuation in response to tele-operator inputs.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The NGHE solution shall transmit remote equipment state feedback to the tele-operator.
refined_fr.notes: The original FR is composite. Decomposition into five sub-FRs reflects the teleoperation communication loop. Each sub-FR is atomic and solution-neutral. The latency acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) remains a constraint on the system design, not a functional requirement. Safety governance is addressed through worksite safety plan and operational policies (context constraint), not embedded in the FR itself.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and sensor feedback remain within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget under agreed network conditions.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel redundant communication architecture (primary + failover) with independent latency paths and automatic switchover logic to maintain safe teleoperation even under partial network degradation.

DP3: Deploy a hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of communication latency) and operator-in-the-loop supervisory commands, decoupling safety-critical decisions from round-trip latency.

DP4: Implement a buffered command-response protocol with predictive state synchronization and jitter compensation to mask network variability and present a consistent latency envelope to the operator.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Deploy a hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous safety enforcement at the remote site (independent of communication latency) and operator-in-the-loop supervisory commands, decoupling safety-critical decisions from round-trip latency.

potential_sources: DP3 assumes clear separation between safety-critical logic (local, deterministic) and supervisory control (remote, latency-tolerant). Risk: if safety rules and operator intent become entangled, coupling re-emerges.

DP3 requires reliable state synchronization between remote site and operator console. Risk: if synchronization protocol is tightly coupled to specific network conditions, portability across different network topologies is compromised.

DP3 depends on worksite safety plan and operational policies being well-defined and implementable in local autonomous logic. Risk: ambiguous or evolving safety policies will force frequent re-architecture of local enforcement.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 is preferred because it decouples the FR (safe remote operation) from the specific latency constraint by shifting safety burden to local autonomous enforcement rather than relying solely on communication speed. This is more robust than DP1 (which treats latency as the primary safety lever) and more practical than DP2 (which adds cost and complexity without addressing root cause). DP4 is a supporting tactic but not a primary DP. DP3 + DP1 (latency monitoring) form a complementary pair.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be formally specified and encoded into local autonomous safety logic. Risk: If policies are vague, context-dependent, or frequently revised, local enforcement becomes brittle.

Assumption: Remote site has sufficient computational and sensing capability to enforce safety rules autonomously without operator input. Risk: If remote site is resource-constrained or lacks critical sensors, local enforcement is incomplete.

Assumption: Operator training and procedures ensure supervisory commands are issued within the bounds of local safety rules. Risk: If operator bypasses or conflicts with local rules, safety is compromised.

Assumption: Network conditions remain within 'agreed' bounds (p95 ≤ 150 ms). Risk: If actual network conditions degrade beyond agreement, latency-sensitive operations may fail; DP3 mitigates but does not eliminate this risk.

Assumption: State synchronization between remote site and operator console is reliable and detectable when out-of-sync. Risk: Silent synchronization failures could lead to operator making decisions based on stale state.
Assumption: Failover and recovery mechanisms (if local autonomy fails) are defined and tested. Risk: Undefined recovery paths could leave the system in an unsafe state.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable safe remote operation by enforcing safety-critical constraints autonomously at the remote site and permitting operator supervisory control with teleoperation latency p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions. This revision makes explicit that safety is not solely a function of latency but of a layered control architecture. Pair DP3 with DP1 (latency monitoring and QoS) as a supporting mechanism to detect and alert when latency approaches unsafe bounds, triggering fallback to fully autonomous mode or operator-initiated shutdown.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates hierarchical safety control logic, local autonomous enforcement mechanisms, safety state machines, and operator-in-the-loop supervisory command validation. Owns all safety-critical decision-making independent of communication latency.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services emits safety alerts and status updates to Teleoperation and Vehicle subsystems when autonomous enforcement is triggered or safety state changes.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services receives operator commands and supervisory directives from Teleoperation; validates and gates them against safety constraints before forwarding to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services consumes vehicle state telemetry (position, velocity, sensor readings) to inform local autonomous safety decisions and enforce constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services monitors network latency and link status to trigger fallback to autonomous enforcement when communication is degraded.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of hierarchical safety control, autonomous enforcement, and operator-in-the-loop validation. The DP's core logic (decoupling safety from latency, local autonomous decisions) maps directly to this module's responsibility. Minimal diffusion: only standard safety alert/telemetry interfaces cross boundaries.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the actuator/executor, not the safety decision authority. Placing hierarchical safety control in Vehicle would blur the separation of concerns: Vehicle should execute commands, not own safety policy. High diffusion: safety logic would leak into vehicle firmware, complicating updates and testing.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is the operator interface and command origination point, not the safety enforcement layer. Placing autonomous safety enforcement in Teleoperation conflates operator intent with safety constraints. High diffusion: safety logic would be entangled with UI/UX and network communication handling.

Moderate fit but overly broad. NGHE Solution is the system-level container. While it could logically contain Safety Services, assigning the DP directly to NGHE Solution would bypass the more specific Safety Services module, reducing architectural clarity and making ownership ambiguous.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the dedicated safety-critical subsystem and the natural owner of hierarchical control logic with local autonomous enforcement. The DP's requirement to decouple safety decisions from communication latency aligns precisely with Safety Services' responsibility to maintain safety invariants independent of operator commands or network state. Operator-in-the-loop supervisory validation is a gating function that Safety Services performs on incoming Tasking commands. Telemetry and Network Link interfaces provide the inputs needed for autonomous decision-making.
containment_risks: Risk: If Safety Services does not have direct access to vehicle state telemetry, it cannot make autonomous decisions. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface is routed directly to Safety Services, not mediated through Teleoperation.

Risk: If operator commands (Tasking) are not validated by Safety Services before reaching Vehicle, supervisory gating is bypassed. Mitigation: Enforce that all Tasking flows through Safety Services for validation.

Risk: If network latency monitoring is not integrated into Safety Services, the trigger for fallback to autonomous mode may be delayed. Mitigation: Safety Services must subscribe to Network Link status and implement latency thresholds.

Risk: Hierarchical control logic (local vs. supervisory modes) may be complex; unclear state machine design could lead to unsafe transitions. Mitigation: Formalize safety state machine and validate against safety requirements.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR's core requirement: enabling safe remote operation despite latency constraints. The hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous safety enforcement decouples safety-critical decisions from round-trip latency, satisfying the functional need. The operator-in-the-loop supervisory layer preserves operator authority while the autonomous layer ensures safety invariants hold independently of communication state. This is a sound logical solution to the problem.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface routing. If vehicle state telemetry is not routed directly to Safety Services but instead flows through Teleoperation first, Safety Services' autonomous decision-making will inherit Teleoperation's latency and network dependencies, undermining the DP's core decoupling logic. Mitigation: Enforce direct Telemetry interface from Vehicle to Safety Services; Teleoperation receives a copy for display only.

Risk: Tasking command gating. If operator commands bypass Safety Services validation and flow directly to Vehicle, the supervisory gating function is circumvented. Mitigation: Implement architectural constraint that all Tasking commands from Teleoperation must pass through Safety Services for validation before reaching Vehicle.

Risk: Network Link monitoring integration. If Safety Services does not actively monitor Network Link status and latency metrics, the trigger for fallback to autonomous-only mode may be delayed or missed, leaving the system in a hybrid state longer than safe. Mitigation: Safety Services must subscribe to Network Link status updates and implement explicit latency threshold logic (e.g., if p95 latency exceeds threshold, transition to autonomous-only mode).

Risk: Safety state machine complexity. The hierarchical control logic (local autonomous vs. operator-in-the-loop supervisory modes) introduces state transitions that must be formally validated. Unclear or ad-hoc state machine design could lead to unsafe mode transitions or race conditions. Mitigation: Formalize the safety state machine (e.g., using state diagrams or formal methods) and validate against safety requirements before implementation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services is the optimal owner of DP3 with a containment score of 0.95 and diffusion score of 0.05. The module's responsibility—encapsulating hierarchical safety control logic, local autonomous enforcement mechanisms, and operator-in-the-loop supervisory validation—maps directly to the DP's intent. The four external interfaces (SafetyAlert, Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal, indicating clean architectural boundaries. No evidence of DP logic leaking into Vehicle, Teleoperation, or other modules. The ownership is clear and unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR's acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) is measurable and testable. Safety Services' autonomous enforcement can be validated through simulation and hardware-in-the-loop testing by injecting latency faults and verifying that safety invariants hold. Operator-in-the-loop supervisory commands can be tested by injecting network delays and confirming that Safety Services correctly gates or rejects commands that would violate safety constraints. The hierarchical control architecture is amenable to formal verification of state machine correctness.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The allocation of DP3 to Safety Services is explicit and unambiguous. The module boundary clearly owns hierarchical safety control, autonomous enforcement, and supervisory validation. The four interfaces (SafetyAlert, Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link) define how Safety Services interacts with other subsystems. Ownership of each interface is clear: Safety Services is the consumer of Tasking and Telemetry, the producer of SafetyAlert, and the monitor of Network Link. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for safety-critical decisions or fallback logic.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the safety state machine for hierarchical control (autonomous vs. supervisory modes) with explicit transition conditions, guard clauses, and timeout logic. Document the state diagram and validate against safety requirements.

Specify the latency threshold (e.g., p95 > 150 ms or absolute timeout) that triggers transition from supervisory to autonomous-only mode. This threshold should be derived from the worksite safety plan and operational policies.
Enforce architectural constraint: all Tasking commands from Teleoperation must flow through Safety Services for validation before reaching Vehicle. Document this in the interface specification.

Enforce architectural constraint: vehicle state telemetry must be routed directly to Safety Services (not mediated through Teleoperation) to ensure autonomous decision-making is not dependent on Teleoperation's latency or availability.

Define the fallback behavior when Safety Services detects network degradation: specify which operator commands are allowed, which are gated, and which trigger autonomous enforcement. Document in the safety state machine.

Add acceptance test: inject network latency faults (e.g., p95 > 150 ms) and verify that Safety Services correctly transitions to autonomous-only mode and maintains safety invariants without operator intervention.
Add acceptance test: verify that operator commands that violate safety constraints are rejected by Safety Services even when network latency is low, confirming supervisory gating logic.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP3 (hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous safety enforcement and operator-in-the-loop supervisory validation) is logically sound and well-allocated to Safety Services with minimal diffusion risk (0.05). However, the gate results identify critical architectural constraints and state machine formalization requirements that must be decomposed into next-level sub-FRs to ensure safe realization. The recommended revisions—particularly formalizing the safety state machine, specifying latency thresholds, enforcing command gating, and validating telemetry routing—are not minor implementation details but represent distinct functional requirements that warrant explicit decomposition. Given the high safety criticality, proceeding directly to implementation without decomposing these constraints into verifiable sub-FRs would violate the principle of hierarchical design clarity and traceability. Decomposition will ensure each constraint is owned, validated, and testable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Formalize and validate the hierarchical safety control state machine (autonomous vs. supervisory modes) with explicit transition conditions, guard clauses, and timeout logic to ensure safe mode transitions and prevent race conditions.

FR3.2: Specify and enforce the latency threshold (e.g., p95 > 150 ms) that triggers transition from operator-in-the-loop supervisory mode to autonomous-only mode, derived from worksite safety plan and operational policies.

FR3.3: Enforce architectural constraint that all operator Tasking commands from Teleoperation must flow through Safety Services for validation and gating before reaching Vehicle, preventing bypass of supervisory safety enforcement.

FR3.4: Enforce architectural constraint that vehicle state telemetry must be routed directly from Vehicle to Safety Services (not mediated through Teleoperation) to ensure autonomous decision-making is independent of Teleoperation's latency or availability.
FR3.5: Implement active monitoring of Network Link status and latency metrics within Safety Services to detect network degradation and trigger timely transition to autonomous-only mode.

FR3.6: Define and document fallback behavior when Safety Services detects network degradation, specifying which operator commands are allowed, which are gated, and which trigger autonomous enforcement.

FR3.7: Validate through acceptance testing that Safety Services correctly transitions to autonomous-only mode when network latency exceeds threshold and maintains safety invariants without operator intervention.
FR3.8: Validate through acceptance testing that operator commands violating safety constraints are rejected by Safety Services even when network latency is low, confirming supervisory gating logic.

revise.why: No revisions to current level required. FR3, DP3, and Safety Services module allocation are sound. Decomposition addresses the gap between the validated DP and safe realization by making architectural constraints and state machine requirements explicit as sub-FRs.

assumptions: Latency threshold (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is achievable under nominal network conditions and will be formally specified in FR3.2.
Vehicle's autonomous safety enforcement capabilities (e.g., emergency stop, geofence enforcement) are sufficient to maintain safety invariants without operator intervention.
Network Link monitoring is available and can provide real-time latency metrics (p95) to Safety Services.
Teleoperation and Vehicle subsystems can be modified to enforce the architectural constraints (command gating, direct telemetry routing) without introducing new safety risks.
Safety state machine can be formalized and validated using standard methods (state diagrams, formal verification) before implementation.
open_risks: Risk: State machine complexity. Hierarchical control with mode transitions (supervisory ↔ autonomous) introduces complexity that could harbor race conditions or unsafe transitions if not formally validated. Mitigation: Decompose into FR3.1 with explicit formalization requirement.

Risk: Latency threshold derivation. The threshold (p95 > 150 ms) must be justified by worksite safety analysis and operational policies. If threshold is set incorrectly (too high or too low), system may fail to transition to autonomous mode when needed or transition prematurely, causing operational disruption. Mitigation: Decompose into FR3.2 with explicit derivation and validation requirement.

Risk: Telemetry routing enforcement. If architectural constraint (direct telemetry routing) is not enforced during implementation, Safety Services' autonomous decisions will inherit Teleoperation's latency, undermining DP3's core decoupling logic. Mitigation: Decompose into FR3.4 with explicit architectural constraint and interface specification.

Risk: Command gating bypass. If Tasking commands can bypass Safety Services validation, supervisory safety enforcement is circumvented. Mitigation: Decompose into FR3.3 with explicit architectural constraint and interface specification.

Risk: Network Link monitoring integration. If Safety Services does not actively monitor Network Link status, fallback to autonomous-only mode may be delayed or missed. Mitigation: Decompose into FR3.5 with explicit monitoring and threshold logic requirement.

Risk: Acceptance test coverage. If acceptance tests (FR3.7, FR3.8) do not adequately cover latency injection, mode transitions, and command gating, unsafe behavior may not be detected before deployment. Mitigation: Develop comprehensive test plans with explicit fault injection scenarios.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of the hierarchical safety control state machine (state diagram, transition table, guard clauses, timeout logic) for FR3.1.
Derivation document justifying the latency threshold (p95 > 150 ms) based on worksite safety analysis, vehicle dynamics, and operator reaction time for FR3.2.
Architectural constraint specification and interface design document enforcing command gating (FR3.3) and direct telemetry routing (FR3.4).
Network Link monitoring specification (metrics, update frequency, threshold logic) for FR3.5.
Fallback behavior specification (decision table or state machine) defining allowed/gated/autonomous-triggered commands for FR3.6.
Acceptance test plans with fault injection scenarios (latency injection, network outages, command validation) for FR3.7 and FR3.8.
Formal verification report (e.g., model checking, theorem proving) validating state machine correctness and safety invariant preservation for FR3.1.
Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) test results demonstrating safe mode transitions and autonomous enforcement under simulated latency faults.
